narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile includes a solid LSAT score of 36, an undergraduate GPA of 3.0, and Year 1 school decile ranking of 3rd out of 10. Your Year 3 decile ranking was also in the 3rd position, which placed you below the typical student's standing around the 5th or 6th decile in both cases. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 61.8% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This threshold reflects the school's aim to prioritize students with stronger signals of bar exam success, as past data shows those who fail often struggle in early law school coursework. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the lower third of your class signaled potential challenges in handling advanced legal studies compared to peers who ranked higher. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since your 36 is close to the average of 36.7 and demonstrates solid reasoning ability. Your Year 1 decile ranking also contributed toward the failure side, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance from the start of your studies. Overall, while your LSAT helped balance things out, the lower decile rankings in both early and later years stood out as the biggest drags when compared to typical admitted students who often rank in the middle or upper half of their classes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from a supportive background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger study habits or additional coursework, retake the LSAT if feasible to push above average, and consider part-time programs or gap-year experiences that build a track record of high performance. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly in your favor.
